title: Mahābhārata: Strīparvan
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE (whole epic); this book belongs to the older narrative core
summary: The eleventh book of the Mahābhārata, the 'Book of the Women.' Following the catastrophic battle at Kurukṣetra, it depicts the lamentation of the women of both armies as they survey the battlefield strewn with the dead. Queen Gāndhārī, mother of the slain Kauravas, surveys the carnage and curses Kṛṣṇa, and Vidura and Vyāsa offer consolation on grief and the inevitability of death.
history: The Mahābhārata grew over centuries from a martial core into an encyclopedic epic, traditionally ascribed to the sage Vyāsa but in fact a composite, anonymous accretion. The Strīparvan is one of the shorter books and includes the Strīvilāpa (lament) and the Śrāddha/Jalapradānika sections on funeral rites. The Pune Critical Edition (1919-1966) established its standard text.

title: Mahābhārata: Śāntiparvan
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
genre: epic (parvan / book of the Mahābhārata); didactic 'wisdom literature'
date_estimate: largely a later didactic stratum, c. 2nd c. BCE - 4th c. CE
summary: The twelfth and longest book of the Mahābhārata, the 'Book of Peace.' After the war, the dying Bhīṣma, lying on a bed of arrows, instructs the grief-stricken king Yudhiṣṭhira at length on rājadharma (the duties of kings and good governance), āpaddharma (conduct in times of distress), and mokṣadharma (the path to liberation). It is an encyclopedic treatise embedding philosophy (Sāṃkhya, Yoga), cosmology, fables, and ethics.
history: The Śāntiparvan, with the following Anuśāsanaparvan, is widely regarded as a large later interpolation in the 'wisdom literature' genre, expanding the epic into a compendium of dharma. The mokṣadharma section in particular preserves important early Vaiṣṇava and philosophical material. The critical edition contains roughly 13,000 verses across some 350 chapters.

title: Mahābhārata: Anuśāsanaparvan
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
genre: epic (parvan / book of the Mahābhārata); didactic dharmaśāstra material
date_estimate: later didactic stratum, c. 2nd c. BCE - 4th c. CE
summary: The thirteenth book, the 'Book of Instructions,' continues Bhīṣma's discourse to Yudhiṣṭhira begun in the Śāntiparvan. It covers a vast range of dharma: gift-giving (dāna), vows, the duties of the varṇas, the conduct of women, the merits of pilgrimage and ritual, the greatness of brahmins, and the worship of Śiva and Viṣṇu, before narrating Bhīṣma's final passing.
history: Together with the Śāntiparvan, the Anuśāsanaparvan forms the great didactic core of the epic and is treated as a later expansion that absorbed much normative (dharmaśāstric) and devotional material. It contains the Viṣṇusahasranāma (Thousand Names of Viṣṇu), one of the most popular Hindu devotional texts.

title: Mahābhārata: Aśvamedhikaparvan
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE; contains both narrative and later didactic material
summary: The fourteenth book, the 'Book of the Horse Sacrifice.' Yudhiṣṭhira, to expiate the slaughter of the war, performs the royal aśvamedha (horse sacrifice); the consecrated horse roams the earth followed by Arjuna, who fights numerous kings to assert imperial sovereignty. The book famously embeds the Anugītā, in which Kṛṣṇa restates spiritual teachings to Arjuna, echoing the Bhagavadgītā.
history: The Aśvamedhikaparvan mixes older narrative (Arjuna's campaign, the revival of Parikṣit) with later philosophical insertions such as the Anugītā. The legend of the mongoose at Yudhiṣṭhira's sacrifice, questioning the value of ostentatious ritual, is one of its noted moral episodes.

title: Mahābhārata: Āśramavāsikaparvan
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE
summary: The fifteenth book, the 'Book of the Hermitage.' The aged Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Gāndhārī, Kuntī, and Vidura renounce the kingdom and retire to the forest to live as ascetics. Vyāsa miraculously raises the spirits of the war dead from the Ganges so the bereaved may see them once more; the elders later perish in a forest fire, and Yudhiṣṭhira learns of their deaths.
history: One of the shorter, later books of the epic, it dramatizes the vānaprastha (forest-dweller) stage of life and the theme of renunciation that pervades the post-war books. The 'raising of the dead' episode is among its most striking narrative set-pieces.

title: Mahābhārata: Mausalaparvan
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE
summary: The sixteenth and one of the shortest books, the 'Book of Clubs.' It narrates the self-destruction of the Yādava/Vṛṣṇi clan at Prabhāsa, where, fulfilling Gāndhārī's curse, the Yādavas slaughter one another in a drunken brawl using clubs grown from a cursed iron pestle. Balarāma departs by yoga, and Kṛṣṇa is mortally wounded by the hunter Jarā; the city of Dvārakā is swallowed by the sea.
history: This book brings the divine actors of the epic to their end and sets the stage for the Pāṇḍavas' own renunciation. Its theme of the inexorable fulfillment of curses and the passing of the heroic age marks the onset of the Kali Yuga in epic chronology.

title: Mahābhārata: Mahāprasthānikaparvan
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE
summary: The seventeenth and shortest book, the 'Book of the Great Journey.' The Pāṇḍavas and Draupadī renounce the kingdom and set out on a final pilgrimage toward the Himālayas and Mount Meru, seeking heaven. One by one Draupadī and four of the brothers fall and die along the way, each owing to a moral flaw, leaving only Yudhiṣṭhira, accompanied by a faithful dog, to continue.
history: A brief, deeply moral coda to the epic, it allegorizes the consequences of each character's subtle imperfections. It leads directly into the final book, the Svargārohaṇaparvan.

title: Mahābhārata: Svargārohaṇaparvan
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE
summary: The eighteenth and final book, the 'Book of the Ascent to Heaven.' Yudhiṣṭhira, the lone survivor, refuses to abandon the dog accompanying him (revealed as the god Dharma) and is taken to heaven. There he is tested by an illusory vision of his brothers in hell and the Kauravas in heaven, learns the relativity of reward and punishment, and is finally reunited with all in their true celestial forms.
history: This short concluding book resolves the epic's meditation on dharma, loyalty, and the nature of heaven and hell, and ends with the phalaśruti (statement of the merit of hearing the epic). It completes the eighteen-parvan structure of the whole.

title: Nārāyaṇīya
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: epic episode; theological / devotional treatise embedded in the Mahābhārata
date_estimate: relatively late addition to the epic, c. 3rd-4th c. CE (disputed)
summary: A self-contained theological section (roughly Mahābhārata 12.321-339, the Mokṣadharma sub-parva of the Śāntiparvan) of about 1,000 verses. It expounds the supremacy of Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa, the doctrine of his vyūhas (cosmic emanations), the practice of single-minded devotion (ekāntika-bhakti), and a frame narrative of the sage Nārada's visit to Śvetadvīpa, the 'White Island' of pure devotees.
history: The Nārāyaṇīya is a key witness to early Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇavism and the formation of bhakti theology within the epic; scholars treat it as one of the latest didactic strata of the Śāntiparvan. It has attracted major Indological study (e.g., the Schreiner-led Tübingen project) as a window onto pre-Gupta and Gupta-era Vaiṣṇava religion.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 1. Bālakāṇḍa
author: Vālmīki (traditional); Bālakāṇḍa largely a later addition
date_estimate: core epic c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE; Bālakāṇḍa among the latest strata
summary: The first book, the 'Book of Childhood.' It narrates the divine plan for Rāma's birth as an avatāra of Viṣṇu to destroy Rāvaṇa, the births of Rāma and his brothers to King Daśaratha, their tutelage under the sage Viśvāmitra and slaying of demons, and Rāma's breaking of Śiva's bow to win Sītā in marriage. It also frames the whole poem with the story of Vālmīki's composition of the first śloka.
history: Books 2-6 form the oldest core of the Rāmāyaṇa, while the Bālakāṇḍa (Book 1) and Uttarakāṇḍa (Book 7) are widely regarded by scholars as later additions, on grounds of style, language, and the more pronounced divinization of Rāma; both are absent or attenuated in the oldest manuscript traditions. The work is traditionally ascribed to the ādikavi ('first poet') Vālmīki.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 3. Araṇyakāṇḍa
author: Vālmīki (traditional)
date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
summary: The third book, the 'Book of the Forest.' It covers Rāma, Sītā, and Lakṣmaṇa's life of exile in the Daṇḍaka forest, their encounters with sages and demons, the disfiguring of the demoness Śūrpaṇakhā, the slaying of Khara and Dūṣaṇa, and the pivotal abduction of Sītā by the demon-king Rāvaṇa after the ruse of the golden deer, ending with Rāma's grief-stricken search.
history: The Araṇyakāṇḍa belongs to the ancient narrative core of the epic (Books 2-6) attributed to Vālmīki. It contains the turning point of the entire story, the abduction of Sītā, which sets in motion the war against Laṅkā.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 3. Araṇyakāṇḍa
author: Vālmīki (traditional)
genre: epic (kāṇḍa / book of the Rāmāyaṇa); recensional/variant text witness
date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
summary: A second text-witness of the third book of the Rāmāyaṇa, the 'Book of the Forest' (Araṇyakāṇḍa). Its content is the same as GE09ram_03_u: the forest exile, the episode with Śūrpaṇakhā, the slaying of Khara, the golden-deer ruse, and Rāvaṇa's abduction of Sītā. The differing work_id suffix ('pu') indicates a distinct recension or pāda-by-pāda/electronic encoding of the same kāṇḍa.
history: The Rāmāyaṇa survives in several regional recensions (notably Northern and Southern), and digital corpora often archive parallel encodings of the same book; this entry appears to be such an alternate version of the Araṇyakāṇḍa. The underlying text remains the Vālmīki Rāmāyaṇa's third book.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 4. Kiṣkindhākāṇḍa
author: Vālmīki (traditional)
date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
summary: The fourth book, the 'Book of Kiṣkindhā.' Rāma allies with the exiled monkey-prince Sugrīva, kills Sugrīva's brother Vālin to restore him to the throne of Kiṣkindhā, and in return the monkey hosts mobilize search parties to find Sītā. It concludes with Hanumān and the southern party learning from Sampāti that Sītā is held captive in Laṅkā.
history: Part of the ancient core of the epic, this book develops the alliance between Rāma and the vānaras and foregrounds Hanumān, whose devotion becomes central to later Rāma bhakti. The Vālin-slaying episode is a noted locus of ethical debate in the commentarial tradition.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 5. Sundarakaṇḍa
author: Vālmīki (traditional)
date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
summary: The fifth book, the 'Beautiful Book.' It centers on Hanumān's heroic leap across the ocean to Laṅkā, his search through Rāvaṇa's city, his discovery of the imprisoned Sītā in the Aśoka grove, his delivery of Rāma's signet ring, the burning of Laṅkā with his flaming tail, and his return to report Sītā's whereabouts to Rāma.
history: The Sundarakāṇḍa is the most beloved and ritually recited book of the Rāmāyaṇa, prized for its devotional focus on Hanumān and traditionally regarded as especially auspicious. It belongs to the epic's ancient core.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 6. Yuddhakāṇḍa
author: Vālmīki (traditional)
date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
summary: The sixth book, the 'Book of War' (also called Laṅkākāṇḍa). It narrates the building of the bridge (setu) to Laṅkā, the great battle between Rāma's monkey army and Rāvaṇa's demons, the deaths of Kumbhakarṇa, Indrajit, and finally Rāvaṇa, the rescue of Sītā and her trial by fire (agniparīkṣā), and the triumphal return to Ayodhyā for Rāma's coronation.
history: The climactic and longest action book of the epic's ancient core, it brings the central narrative to its resolution. In most recensions the original story effectively concludes here, with the Uttarakāṇḍa regarded as a later appendix.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 7. Uttarakāṇḍa
author: Vālmīki (traditional); widely regarded as a later addition
date_estimate: latest stratum of the epic, c. 1st-4th c. CE (later than Books 2-6)
summary: The seventh and final book, the 'Later Book.' It recounts events after Rāma's coronation: the history and genealogy of Rāvaṇa and the rākṣasas, Hanumān's origins, and—most famously—Rāma's banishment of the pregnant Sītā in response to public doubt of her chastity. Sītā takes refuge with Vālmīki, bears the twins Lava and Kuśa, and finally returns to the earth, after which Rāma departs the world.
history: The Uttarakāṇḍa is, with the Bālakāṇḍa, the part of the Rāmāyaṇa most consistently judged by scholars to be a later interpolation, on grounds of its more explicit divinization of Rāma, narrative inconsistencies, and absence from the oldest manuscripts. Its treatment of Sītā's exile has been a major focus of later literary, devotional, and critical engagement.

title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 1
author: Ānandavardhana
date_estimate: c. mid-to-late 9th c. CE (Ānandavardhana fl. c. 820-890 CE, Kashmir)
summary: The first chapter (uddyota) of the Dhvanyāloka ('Light on Suggestion'), the foundational work of the dhvani school of Sanskrit poetics. It introduces and defends the thesis that dhvani—suggested or implied meaning—is the very soul (ātman) of poetry, distinguishing the literal/denoted sense from the more powerful suggested sense, against rival theorists who deny suggestion's existence.
history: Ānandavardhana, a court scholar under King Avantivarman of Kashmir, established dhvani as the dominant theory of Indian aesthetics. A debate (going back to Abhinavagupta) holds that the kārikās may be by an earlier 'Dhvanikāra' (called Sahṛdaya, hence the alternate title Sahṛdayāloka) while Ānandavardhana wrote the prose vṛtti; others ascribe the whole to him. Abhinavagupta's commentary, the Locana, became its classic exposition.

title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 2
author: Ānandavardhana
date_estimate: c. mid-to-late 9th c. CE
summary: The second chapter of the Dhvanyāloka. It develops a typology of suggestion, treating in particular the relation of dhvani to rasa (aesthetic emotion), the varieties of suggested meaning, and the way figures of speech (alaṃkāras), qualities (guṇas), and rasa-dhvani operate. It elaborates the famous doctrine that the suggestion of rasa is the highest kind of dhvani.
history: Part of Ānandavardhana's four-uddyota treatise that reoriented Sanskrit poetics around suggestion and made rasa-dhvani its central category, profoundly influencing Abhinavagupta, Mammaṭa, and the whole later tradition.

title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 3
author: Ānandavardhana
date_estimate: c. mid-to-late 9th c. CE
summary: The third and longest chapter of the Dhvanyāloka. It analyzes suggestion in fine grain, distinguishing the suggestion of bare matter, of figures, and of rasa; it discusses how the suggested meaning is conveyed by the power of words and sense, the relation between principal and subordinate suggestion (guṇībhūta-vyaṅgya), and the proper deployment of figures, qualities, and styles in service of rasa.
history: This chapter contains much of the technical machinery of dhvani theory and its applications to literary composition, and was extensively commented upon by Abhinavagupta in his Locana.

title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 4
author: Ānandavardhana
date_estimate: c. mid-to-late 9th c. CE
summary: The fourth and concluding chapter of the Dhvanyāloka. It addresses the inexhaustible creativity of poetry under the theory of suggestion, arguing that despite the antiquity of poetic themes, dhvani allows infinite novelty, and it discusses the poet's genius (pratibhā), originality versus borrowing, and the unlimited scope that suggestion opens for the imaginative writer.
history: The final uddyota rounds off Ānandavardhana's argument by celebrating the boundless expressive potential of poetry grounded in suggestion, securing the Dhvanyāloka's status as the cornerstone of classical Indian literary theory.
